data.title: Stop Buying British American Tobacco And Start Buying Altria
data.description:
When it comes to investing, it seems like there are - broadly - two schools of portfolio management.

On the one hand, some investors think that buying and holding high-quality companies over the long-term is the best route to building wealth, and it’s hard to argue with the results of top investors like Warren Buffett, who generally employ this approach.

On the other hand, many in the markets believe that continually optimizing a portfolio, in light of new information, is the best way to build wealth. Proponents of this camp argue that as stock prices and fundamental realities change, so too should your holdings.

Here at PropNotes, we fall squarely into the second camp. While it’s true that buying high-quality companies and closing your eyes for 20 years has tended to yield great results, we feel as though continually optimizing a portfolio - even when there are tax implications - leads to stronger performance over time.

That is why we continually update our view on stocks as prices move around.

One stock whose price has moved dramatically over the last year is British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI).

Since our initial bullish coverage in 2023, we’ve been pounding the table on the stock, arguing that shares appear massively undervalued, and that fears around tobacco’s demise seem wildly overblown.

As of today, we’ve been proven largely right, with the stock returning more than 125% since our Strong Buy rating in January of last year:

This dwarfs the total return of the S&P 500, and has been a boon for shareholders who followed our rating.

Now, however, 18 months on, we think the stock appears more fully valued, trading at a mid-teens earnings valuation.

In essence, the stock has leapfrogged our projections and caught up with our 2027 price target.

As BTI's share price has increased, the attractiveness of the dividend has also been reduced, proving a negative double whammy for value/income investors at this price.

While we think the company will continue to perform well, we also think the attractiveness of shares is now significantly reduced, and we're downgrading the stock to a Hold.

Today, we'll take a deeper look at our thesis with British American Tobacco, dissect where the valuation sits now, and explain why we think rotating out of British American Tobacco - and into Altria (NYSE:MO) - is the most optimal thing you can do with your money.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Thesis

Let’s start by taking a look at our original thesis for British American Tobacco when we first wrote about the stock in 2023.

In short, our view on BTI was comprised of three prongs.

First off, BTI sells an unbelievable, addictive, high-margin product. As far as businesses go, it’s one of the best business models you can have. Not only are your customers addicted to your product, but regulatory capture prevents new entrants from stealing up market share.

It’s an ideal situation for a business to find itself in, even if the growth rates don’t match other faster, growing sectors like Tech.

Secondly, we thought that the company's dividend of around 10% (at the time) reflected tremendous income potential. Not only is this dividend great for income investors due to the stable, well-backed nature of the business, but 9% in virtually guaranteed income also sets the stage for excellent total returns if the stock appreciates even modestly.

In some ways, we viewed the stock like a low risk, high quality bond - paying a solid yield, with a great underlying business, and having the potential for respectable, organic appreciation.

Finally, we argued that the market was too negative on the stock due to fears around long-term write-offs in the sticks business, in addition to concerns around smoking trends (and bans) in developed countries.

Trading at valuation of around 7.5x to 8x earnings, we thought there was significant upside in the stock, simply due to the potential for mean reversion in the multiple. That is, if the market began to change his mind on BTI's long-term potential, then shares might re-rate higher, driving material gains to shareholders.

Taken together, we thought the thesis in BTI was compelling enough for us to issue a Strong Buy rating.

What’s New?

In some respects, nothing is new when it comes to British American Tobacco.

The company still sells its portfolio of tobacco and tobacco-adjacent products, including vapes, heats, pouches, etc. all over the world. Business is good, and the transition from combustibles to smoke free products (like nicotine pouches) is humming along.

However, very materially, and from a tactical perspective, the stock has become much less appealing after its 100% gain.

On the dividend side, BTI no longer pays a double-digit dividend, with payouts now yielding around 5%. This is still high, but much less attractive in terms of deploying new capital, especially versus high-quality, diversified dividend ETFs like SCHD (which pay nearly 4%).

At the same time, the valuation discrepancy between BTI in early 2024 and the company's long-term historical track record has now almost completely vanished with shares trading at more than 13x earnings.

As you can see on the graph below, a huge percentage of the stock's discount has been erased, which significantly reduces the upside potential in our eyes:

You can see this visually, as the stock's price has caught up to historical norms and implied fair values, which are derived from the company's long term EPS growth rates.

Overall, we believe that BTI - as a company - will likely continue to perform well, but there’s no question that we no longer find shares as attractive as they once were.

There's just not nearly as much of a valuation discrepancy to exploit, and there's virtually no 'mean reversion' upside to shares from here.

Where We Go From Here

The real question now is what investors should do going forward.

As we mentioned earlier, some will likely look at their low cost-basis in the stock and continue holding, even if the upside potential has been reduced. For investors in recent years, their yield on cost is probably excellent, especially if they got shares under $30.

However, for our part, we’re interested in having our capital always allocated in places where we think it stands the highest chance to produce strong, continued, total returns.

With BTI, that's no longer the case.

As a result, we think rotating your money from British American Tobacco into Altria could be a smart move.

MO, another tobacco giant, has performed very similarly to BTI over the last few years.

We came out as bullish on Altria in April of 2024, and the stock has appreciated roughly 71% since then - again, far outpacing the market:

However, the reason we think Altria appears more attractive than BTI is for two key reasons.

First off, the dividend, while not incredibly higher, is still above 6%. This additional point of yield could make a difference, depending on your income goals and needs.

Secondly, MO's valuation remains in the low teens, and over the last decade, the stock's average P/E is even higher than BTI's, averaging roughly 15x or more:

As a result, this means you're getting into a stock with more upside potential remaining, with more in the way of yield as well.

This appears preferable vs. BTI.

We think this move makes sense - especially for BTI investors sitting on gains - because you’re getting a company with a similar overall economic profile and business model i.e. selling cigarettes, pouches, vapes, heats, etc, but with today’s prices, you’re baking in more upside and more income.

As a result of this comparison, we’re downgrading BTI to a Hold while remaining bullish on Altria.

How We’re Wrong

Of course, some proponents of British American Tobacco may point to specific brand successes at the company, including the recent success of Velo. Similarly, some BTI investors may prefer the company's ever so marginally higher projected growth rate, which tops 5% (as opposed to MO's 3.7%).

To us, this slim difference in projected EPS growth matters far less than the lower yield and reduced appreciation potential. As a result, MO looks like a strict upgrade when looking across a portfolio.

Summary

As a result of these recent moves, we're downgrading BTI to a Hold, while keeping our bullish Buy stance on Altria

None of these moves reflect issues, questions, or concerns we have about either company, but within the context of portfolio management, we feel the desire to be tactical when simple switches could yield better results over the short, medium and long-term.

Cheers

data.title: Hyliion: The Recent Rebound Seems Unwarranted
data.description:
Hyliion Holdings (NYSE:HYLN) went public back during the special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC") craze of 2020. For a time there, it seemed that just about any novel technology related to electric vehicles, alternative energy, and related fields would obtain a tremendous market valuation. People were in the mood to put their skepticism aside and take the optimistic view on any potential technological breakthrough in the green energy space.

Hyliion was initially a winner from that euphoria. HYLN stock popped from that $10 SPAC offering price to as much as $50 per share at one point. Reportedly, Hyliion founder and CEO Thomas Healy became America's youngest self-made billionaire at the time that the deal went through thanks to his ownership position in suddenly red-hot HYLN stock.

However, the early Hyliion fortunes wouldn't last. In fact, shares are now down more than 95% from the all-time highs, though the stock has meaningfully bounced from its 52-week low of $1.11:

In conjunction with the collapse in the stock price, Hyliion has also changed business models. It has stopped efforts to commercialize the Hypertruck ERX drivetrain system. At the time of the SPAC deal, Hyliion had believed that the Hypertruck ERX could offer significant cost savings to trucking companies while also having a positive impact on environmental sustainability.

Hyliion, in its SPAC presentation, projected that it would generate more than $2 billion in revenues in the year 2024, along with approximately $600 million in EBITDA primarily due to its Hypertruck ERX system. Instead, the company brought in a grand total of $1.5 million of revenue in its actual fiscal year 2024.

Hyliion's New Bet: KARNO

The company has now pivoted to KARNO, a linear generator technology. As per Hyliion's explanation, it initially acquired KARNO in an attempt to bolster the capabilities of its powertrain, but are now attempting to sell it as its own standalone product. See this explanation from a recent Hyliion 10-Q:

"The KARNO generator emerged out of General Electric’s long-running research and development investments in aerospace and metal additive manufacturing across multiple industries and in areas such as generator thermal and performance design.

We initially envisioned utilizing the KARNO generator as new range-extending power source for the Hypertruck powertrain system, given its ability to operate on a wide range of fuel sources, including natural gas and hydrogen.

After the previously announced wind down of our powertrain operations, we shifted our focus to the development and commercialization of the KARNO generator and related research and development services that we have undertaken pursuant to contracts with the United States government."

Consider me uncertain that this technology from GE, which was supposed to be incorporated into Hyliion's powertrain system, will turn out to have a great deal of commercial value as a standalone entity. Hyliion sees it having appeal in the current marketplace due to the rapid increase in electricity demand. That said, there is already established competition here, such as from Mainspring which appears to have a first mover advantage in this product space and has established partnerships with leading utility companies such as NextEra Energy (NEE).

And, as it would turn out, unfortunately for Hyliion shareholders, the new KARNO product is also seemingly having issues with its launch and commercial ramp-up. Back in Q1, the company disclosed what it described as two technical hurdles, these were related to linear electric motor manufacturing and the depowdering of a complex printed part. However, management affirmed its full-year 2025 roadmap despite those hurdles.

However, with its most recent quarter, the company disclosed that it is pushing the wider launch of KARNO, beyond some first adopter units, out into 2026. As a result, the company reduced its 2025 full-year revenue guidance to just 5 to 10 million dollars.

It's interesting to consider the peer companies of Hyliion that went public around the same time back in the early 2020s. A good way to check these potential peers is through Seeking Alpha's people also follow feature, which shows where there is high overlap in interest among specific companies. Here is Hyliion's people also follow peers as of this writing:

I find it notable that two of those firms, Nikola and Fisker already went bankrupt, and the other three peers are also struggling, with all of them having stock prices below $2 per share.

Nikola became particularly famous thanks to its charismatic founder, Trevor Milton, who initially appeared like he could form a serious competitor to Tesla's (TSLA) green energy ambitions. But as we later discovered, Nikola's prototype vehicle didn't work as presented, and Milton was later convicted of fraud related to misrepresenting his firm's technological capabilities.

Nikola ultimately tried to pivot, and they even built a working electric vehicle factory in Arizona at one point. But it never amounted to much of anything, and Nikola subsequently filed bankruptcy. Nikola, and many of the other EV and green energy related firms which went public during the SPAC boom were ultimately low-quality companies which resulted in large losses for shareholders.

Hyliion isn't necessarily going to follow Nikola or Fisker's path to bankruptcy. But it came public during a period when especially low-quality firms had an easy path to market. And the same CEO is still in charge of Hyliion today even after the company's Hypertruck ERX abjectly failed to meet the company's SPAC-era projections. As such, we should probably take bullish projections around KARNO with a grain of salt considering both Hyliion's own history and that of other peer companies which went public during the 2021-era SPAC boom.

To put it another way, if Hyllion were going to launch an IPO today, I believe it would garner a modest valuation simply because there's little demonstrated evidence that the business model here is ultimately going to work.

Rather, I think investors are assigning too much residual value to Hyliion because the stock used to trade as high as $50 per share and is now under $2. A trader might think, "How much farther can it fall?"

But I'd urge folks to consider market capitalization rather than share price. People are still valuing this company at a roughly $300 million price tag. I find that to be a large sum for Hyliion given its lack of meaningful revenue generation historically and the additional fact that its new product seems to be already facing commercialization delays.

HYLN Stock Verdict

Hyliion is a prove it story at this point. Perhaps KARNO will turn into a major commercial success. There is certainly increased demand for power generation solutions at the moment. But I'm not personally willing to bet on KARNO until we see some tangible signs of progress in the financial statements.

At this time, I would value Hyliion primarily on the basis of its cash and investments. To the company's credit, it had $109 million of cash and investments on the balance sheet as of June 2025. On the liability side, there is no long-term debt and just $13 million in total liabilities.

The company has 175 million shares of stock outstanding, meaning that there is about $0.62 per share in total net cash here. Hyliion also has some property and machinery along with some long-term investments. If these could be liquidated at book value, then, in theory, the company has fully $1.23 per share in tangible book value. Hyliion is worth at least that 62 cents per share in cash today, and it could be worth up to that $1.23 per share figure though I'd assume some of the property and machinery might sell at a discount in a liquidation sale.

That said, this floor value will likely erode over time. For context, 12 months ago, Hyliion's tangible book value was $1.54 per share, and it was $2.00 per share back in 2023. The company had negative $47 million in operating cash flow over the past 12 months, suggesting the cash pile would run out sometime around late 2027 at the current pace. Meanwhile, there's not much evidence that the company will become profitable in the near future.

I would not pay anything over tangible book value for Hyliion shares today. And I wouldn't be at all surprised if the stock trades down to its cash value per share over the next few quarters, especially if KARNO continues to have operational setbacks on the road toward potential commercialization. With KARNO facing uncertain commercial prospects and HYLN stock now trading at a wide premium to its cash value per share, I view Hyliion as a sell today.

data.title: Impax Global Opportunities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The portfolio rose in absolute terms but modestly underperformed the MSCI ACWI Index. Strong relative performance came from Information Technology. The small overweight position to the sector was beneficial and stock performance was boosted by enterprise software and semiconductor equipment manufacturing companies. Stocks purchased during heightened volatility earlier in the year, Marvell (MRVL) and ServiceNow (NOW), rebounded after delivering good earnings and contributed to relative performance. Positive performance also came from Consumer Staples, and having no exposure to the Energy sector was also beneficial. Headwinds came from the overweight allocation to Health Care. The sector has been weaker due to proposed healthcare reforms in the US, which some investors believe may lead to a slower pace of spending on drug discovery. Stock selection in Health Care was positive though, and the portfolio has no exposure to the large pharmaceutical companies that have been most under pressure from policy uncertainty. Financials holdings were also weaker on a relative basis. The portfolio faced a style headwind as the rally in the sector was led by lower quality banks that are seen as primary beneficiaries of sector deregulation in the US. While the portfolio’s regional banks, KBC (OTCPK:KBCSY) and Cullen/Frost (CFR), rose in absolute terms, they lagged the rally. Insurance companies, where the portfolio is well weighted, were out of focus and trailed the sector.

Market overview

  • Despite considerable volatility, global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI Index, rose over the quarter in US dollar (USD) terms. A weaker dollar, however, reduced returns for non--USD based investors. The quarter was dominated by tariffs, geopolitics, and the impending passage of the “Big Beautiful Bill” (BBB) in the US.
  • President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs were more stringent than expected, triggering a sell-off in risk assets in April. Bond yields initially rose as inflation uncertainty increased. However, markets rallied as China and the US agreed to delay additional punitive tariffs and begin negotiations on a trade deal. While the outlook for inflation remains uncertain, recession fears have faded. US bond yields were also volatile over the quarter but finished almost unchanged, which also supported the rally in equities, and the VIX volatility index finished near its lowest levels.
  • The impeding passage of the “BBB” in the US has generally been supportive of equities as it is expected to lead to further fiscal stimulus, even while it increases US government debt. The earnings season was also broadly better than expected, and the corporate growth outlook remains positive. Markets have been led by more growth-oriented sectors and by large market-cap stocks, while more defensive sectors have lagged. The Health Care sector remains under intense scrutiny as one component of the “BBB” is aimed at moderating healthcare prices.
  • Looking ahead, given current valuations, we expect markets to remain range-bound over the course of the rest of the year, supported by earnings expectations. This should give rise to a fertile investment environment for active managers that are willing to take a selective stance across market capitalisation, sector and region.

Contributors (absolute basis)

  • Oracle (ORCL) (Systems Software, US) reported earnings and revenue growth exceeding analysts' expectations. Customer commitments are projected to double next year, contributing to a significant backlog in calendar year 2026. As the fourth-largest ’hyperscaler’, Oracle disclosed a major cloud services agreement expected to generate over US$30bn in annual revenue. Additionally, the company announced plans to purchase US$40bn-worth of Nvidia chips for collaboration with OpenAI on US data centre capacity, which is expected to drive additional long-term revenue.
  • Microsoft (MSFT) (Systems Software, US) outperformed in Q2, driven by a positive market reaction to strong earnings results. Importantly, results for its cloud computing arm Azure came in well ahead of expectations and there are indications of a re-acceleration of growth.
  • Nvidia (NVDA) (Semiconductors, US) experienced a share price bounce during the period, as strong Q1 results (revenue exceeding estimates, stronger than expected client capital expenditure) and improving policy sentiment provided evidence of their attractive position to benefit from growth in AI infrastructure, and the need for higher efficiencies.

Detractors (absolute basis)

  • Thermo Fisher (TMO) (Life Sciences Tools & Services, US) has faced negative sentiment, as has the healthcare sector at-large, due to the US Administration's attempt to moderate drug pricing. If successful, this could have a negative downstream effect on the tools companies as companies delay equipment orders. However, we believe Thermo Fisher remains attractively valued given its scale and diversified product portfolio.
  • Marsh & McLennan (MMC) (Insurance Brokers, US) after a strong Q1, Marsh & McLennan gave up some of its relative gains on concerns that insurance property pricing was decelerating faster than expected. In addition, Q1 results were weaker than expected: income from their non-care fiduciary business declined, due to lower short-term interest rates, and organic growth rates were lower than expected.
  • Alcon (ALC)(Health Care Supplies, Switzerland) underperformed in Q2 after strong performance in Q1. When the company released Q1 earnings in May, they lowered the top end of their sales guidance range. Having previously had a capital markets day that was positive and well received by investors, this led to disappointment. The US cataract environment was also weaker than had been expected at the beginning of the year, however the investment team believes that revenue growth should improve through the rest of 2025 as new products launch.

Outlook

  • Stock markets rallied in Q2 as trade tensions eased and on the expectation of tax cuts and fiscal stimulus in the US. However, while there has been progress in trade negotiations, there is the distinct possibility of continued geopolitical uncertainty and volatility. Against this backdrop, the portfolio maintains its focus on identifying companies with more durable business models and higher predictability of returns. The investment team is also seeking to maintain a balance between companies with inherently defensive characteristics, that should perform well if volatility returns, and companies with exposure to secular growth opportunities in more cyclical sub-sectors.
  • The portfolio has little exposure to parts of the market that may be most negatively impacted if uncertainty persists. Exposure to consumer discretionary spending is minimal; the portfolio is materially underweight relative to the sector. An inherent leaning towards defensive exposures, such as Health Care, defensive Materials companies, industrial gases and nutritional ingredients, should also benefit the portfolio during periods of volatility.
  • Over the balance of the year, the investment team retain high conviction in several underappreciated opportunities in the portfolio, including beneficiaries of European infrastructure investment, selected opportunities in Health Care – including medical devices used in care settings, which are less affected by policy uncertainty affecting the sector – and higher quality defensive Financials companies which have been out of favour, relative to primary beneficiaries of US deregulation. Finally, the portfolio retains holdings in a number of companies that are able to capture either revenue or cost opportunities through clear AI implementation. Examples include recruitment platforms, data analytics providers, exchange operators and companies leveraging AI in areas such as logistics.

Impax Global Opportunities Fund is a long-only all-cap global equities strategy that invests in high quality companies with sustainability tailwinds.

data.title: Carillon Eagle Small Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Small-cap stocks rebounded in the second quarter, recovering from the broad drawdown at the beginning of the year. Among the style indexes, the Russell 2000® Growth Index (up 11.96%) outperformed the Russell 2000® Value Index (up 4.96%). In the growth index, information technology (up 19.77%) and industrials (up 19.13%) were the top contributors. Materials (up 14.17%) and financials (up 13.81%) also posted solid returns that exceeded the performance of the overall growth index. Consumer discretionary (up 11.13%) fared well during the quarter, outperforming its value counterpart. Communication services (up 8.26%) lagged both the broader index and its value peers. Healthcare (up 3.28%), the largest sector in the index, fell out of favor after outperforming the broader benchmark on a relative basis in the previous quarter. Consumer staples (up 1.89%) and energy (up 1.61%) posted modest gains, unlike their value counterparts that finished in negative territory. Real estate (down 0.80%) and utilities (down 1.11%) were the only sectors to detract from performance, though their lower weights in the index helped mitigate their impact.

Investment Team

Eric Mintz, CFA Managing Director and Portfolio Manager

Christopher Sassouni, D.M.D., Portfolio Manager

David Cavanaugh, Portfolio Manager

Characteristics

Number of holdings: 99

Portfolio Review

Top securities

Shake Shack is a fast-casual restaurant chain offering burgers, hot dogs, crinkle-cut fries, chicken dishes, milkshakes, and other beverages. The stock performed well, driven by strong quarterly margin performance and an upward revision to annual margin guidance. In addition, traffic trends showed notable improvement after poor weather dampened traffic during the first quarter.

Woodward develops and produces control systems and energy conversion solutions and components for the aerospace and industrial end markets. The stock performed well, driven by a quarterly report highlighted by particularly strong performance in the company’s aerospace aftermarket vertical and considerable margin strength. The potential for a long-waited increased rate of production from large airframe original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) could provide an additional tailwind to growth going forward. Investors also remain optimistic about the company’s growing exposure to newer-generation aircraft that is expected to result in a further step-change in aftermarket growth in the latter half of the decade.

ATI (ATI) produces specialty metals and alloys for a diversified set of industries, but most notably aerospace and defense. After a period of somewhat challenging operating results in the past year, the company put together a more impressive quarter that exceeded expectations across the board.

We believe the company’s desirable positioning within an exceptionally tight aerospace specialty alloys market sets it up for a bright future if it can continue executing. We also believe ATI’s exposure to newer-generation aircraft, an accelerating pricing environment, and newly announced contracts with a large OEM customer to which ATI historically has had little exposure all should provide for a positive backdrop going forward.

AeroVironment (AVAV) is a defense technology provider of integrated capabilities across air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Following several quarters of choppy results that caused investors to question the company’s future growth prospects after a swift decline in the revenue contribution from Ukraine’s war with Russia, the shares rebounded largely on the prospects of increased global defense spending in a number of next-generation product areas that the company provides. After a period of some doubt, the company also closed what we view as a transformational acquisition of BlueHalo.

Boot Barn Holdings is a U.S. retailer specializing in western and work footwear, apparel, and accessories through physical stores and online. The stock rebounded sharply after reporting solid quarterly results and providing forward guidance, inclusive of tariffs, that exceeded expectations. Boot Barn’s brand strength continues to reflect its enduring nature, which is underpinned by strong consumer enthusiasm and widespread appeal and reinforced by management’s ability to execute well to offset the impacts of tariffs.

Bottom securities

Aris Water Solutions provides water infrastructure and solutions for the oil and gas industry within the Delaware subbasin of the Permian Basin in West Texas and southeastern New Mexico. The shares declined because of concerns about what the recent downturn in oil prices would mean for drilling activity on the company’s footprint. Despite this ongoing fluid situation, the company’s results remain more tied to ongoing oil production, where it provides an essential service to operators, primarily by handling, disposing of, or recycling volumes of produced water that continue to grow as shale wells mature and the quality of the resource slowly declines.

Viper Energy owns and acquires mineral and royalty interests in oil and natural gas properties primarily in the Permian Basin. The company’s stock was under pressure following the decline in oil prices brought on by global macroeconomic concerns spurred by the ongoing trade war as well as the decision of the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) to bring more production online in the coming months. Despite this, we remain optimistic about the company’s prospects, as it continues to strengthen its position in the minerals space, partly through accretive acquisitions. Additionally, its relationship with its parent company, Diamondback, gives Viper Energy what we regard as unrivaled visibility to future growth, a characteristic that is currently lacking throughout most of the oil and gas industry.

Cellebrite, which provides software to help law enforcement investigators extract and analyze data from smartphones, provided an outlook that was below expectations, as there were delays in closing deals with government customers, both in the United States and abroad. Despite the temporary setback, which was due primarily to leadership changes and unsettled budgets, two secular trends remain intact: one is the use of smartphone data in detecting and prosecuting criminal activity; the other is the need to comply with standards to preserve the chain of custody of evidence collected during investigations.

BellRing produces and sells protein-based nutritional beverages and bars for health- and fitness-oriented consumers. The stock delivered lackluster performance as guidance for the upcoming quarter came in below expectations. We feel the slower growth rate is temporary and a reflection of inventory adjustments at the company’s warehouse club channel partners, and not a sign of more pervasive slowing at BellRing or in the protein shake category.

Merit Medical Systems designs, manufactures, and markets single-use medical devices for interventional, diagnostic, and therapeutic procedures, primarily in cardiology, radiology, oncology, and endoscopy. The stock lagged due largely to tariff concerns following the Liberation Day announcement, impacting sentiment given its global footprint and exposure to China. While earnings results beat expectations, earnings per share (EPS) guidance was lowered due to increased tariff-related costs. Management expects to mitigate about half of this impact by 2026 through manufacturing and operational initiatives. Despite this near-term margin pressure, we believe nothing has fundamentally changed with the company as it continues to exceed revenue guidance. We remain confident in the company’s long-term outlook.

Outlook

The second quarter proved to be one of the most turbulent environments for equity markets in recent memory. Driven by historically high levels of trade policy uncertainty, stocks suffered steep declines in April with most market averages entering bear market territory and investor sentiment surveys reaching extreme levels of bearishness. Markets sharply rebounded as tariffs were paused and appeared likely to be far less punitive than the draconian scenarios introduced on Liberation Day. Earnings season also served as a catalyst to help stocks recover their losses as the anticipated impact of tariffs in many cases turned out to be more manageable than initially feared. Additionally, many of the momentum stocks associated with the investment theme around AI surged as large hyperscalers either reiterated or raised their capital spending plans and provided strong evidence of continued strength in user adoption trends. Looking ahead, we believe the continued uncertainty on trade policy is likely to persist for the foreseeable future but believe the market, for now, appears willing to heavily discount the headlines associated with the current administration’s negotiating tactics. We expect inflationary data to remain relatively favorable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to lower interest rates in the second half of the year. During the coming months, as the market shifts its valuation focus to 2026, we see a reasonable base case for solid earnings growth. Reduced trade and policy uncertainty, potential fiscal stimulus, and lower interest rates are the key assumptions supporting this constructive outlook. These conditions should foster a favorable macroeconomic environment and allow a broadening of the market beyond the AI investment theme. In this backdrop, we believe high-quality secular growth stocks are positioned to perform well.

Cyclicals

The outlook for the cyclical areas of the market appears broadly constructive but selectivity remains key. Within the energy sector, the outlook for natural gas prices appears favorable given rising LNG exports and higher electricity demand from the buildout of data centers. In contrast, oil prices appear likely to remain subdued as OPEC+ – the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, plus 10 other oil-producing countries, including Russia – shifts its strategy to win back market share from North American producers. The outlook for aerospace and defense continues to remain strong. Signs of improvement in manufacturing bottlenecks throughout the aerospace supply chain suggest that the largest aircraft manufacturers appear to be on track to ramp monthly production rates toward their targets. Defense companies should also get a boost from an increasingly tumultuous geopolitical landscape, alongside growing defense spending by European nations as they begin to meet their NATO commitments. Housing remains challenged as mortgage rates remain at levels that constrict affordability and restrain residential construction. The AI data center buildout remains a favorable tailwind for companies throughout the electrical equipment industry as utilities ramp up spending to meet rising demand for power generation. Finally, further resolution of trade policy uncertainty in coming months should help promote investment in U.S. manufacturing, and we believe factory automation spending will be a durable investment theme for the next several years.

Healthcare

The healthcare industry remains the largest sector in the United States, with total annual expenditures reaching $5 trillion and accounting for 17.7% of gross domestic product (GDP). This sector has been growing at an impressive rate of about 5% per year, driven by the aging population and the increasing prevalence of chronic conditions, which are responsible for 75% of healthcare costs. Most individuals over the age of 65 have two or more chronic conditions, and Western medicine often struggles to manage these effectively. Additionally, novel therapies are extending lifespans allowing people to live well into their 80s, but these therapies are expensive and significantly contribute to rising healthcare costs.

Despite the sector’s size and importance, healthcare has underperformed in recent years, generating lackluster returns compared with major benchmarks. The sector has been working off the excesses brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic. The massive deceleration in demand for healthcare products and services after 2021 led to a significant decline in stock prices and, in turn, caused the sector to generate very poor returns on an absolute and relative basis. The surge in initial public offerings (IPOs), particularly of biotechnology companies during 2020 and 2021, reduced the overall quality of drug candidates being developed, resulting in higher than normal negative clinical readouts and investor abandonment. This has caused volatility not only among biotech stocks, but also among healthcare stocks in general. Healthcare is a highly regulated industry, overseen by agencies such as the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, the Food and Drug Administration, and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Regulatory uncertainty, especially with new directors and commissioners being installed, has further deterred investors. Add to that the uncertainty over the provisions in the “One Big Beautiful Bill” that could negatively impact healthcare stocks, creating additional angst among investors.

There are, however, reasons for optimism. Recent mergers and acquisition (M&A) activity has picked up, though it is still below normal levels. Much of the M&A activity has been focused on medical device and biotechnology companies, where larger acquirers are looking to smaller, innovative companies to provide them with new products to enhance their own portfolios and market positions.

From a valuation perspective, healthcare is generally cheaper than most sectors currently. Once investors are comfortable with the impact of tariffs, and other policy and regulatory overhangs, interest in healthcare stocks could see a resurgence. Until then, larger active bets are being placed on predictable, U.S.-centric businesses like major distributors, which have steady margins and generate substantial free cash flow. Interest from generalist investors is expected to continue to trend positively into the second half of the year, following the potential passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill along with the finalization of annual reimbursement schedules by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

Technology

Markets entered the second quarter with renewed volatility, as the announcement of sweeping tariffs in early April triggered a sharp sell-off in equities, particularly in hardware and semiconductor stocks. In contrast, software companies proved to be more resilient, with their recurring revenue models and lower exposure to global supply chains helping to offset the blow. Investor sentiment quickly deteriorated amid fears of a prolonged trade conflict and its potential impact on corporate earnings. However, those concerns have thus far proven largely overdone. As the quarter progressed, greater clarity from the administration on trade negotiations, along with reassuring commentary and results during earnings season, helped ease fears and sparked a strong rebound in stocks. Despite lingering uncertainty, inflation expectations have remained stable, and the prospect of further trade deals have provided additional support for risk assets. In this environment, technology companies with strong balance sheets and consistent cash flow generation continue to stand out as potential investment opportunities. The current administration is also expected to create a more constructive backdrop for a rebound in mergers and acquisitions, as well as a potential revival in IPO activity. Within the technology sector, we continue to identify durable, well-capitalized companies that we believe are poised to benefit from long-term secular trends such as cloud computing, artificial intelligence, mobility and telecommunications infrastructure, digital payments, the Internet of Things, smart homes, industrial automation, security software, e-gaming, and alternative energy.

Financials

The outlook for the financials sector is constructive as economic growth has stayed resilient and fears about growth have thus far been overblown. Interest rates have settled somewhat from the volatility seen earlier in the year. In this environment, we see the most interesting opportunities outside of the typical banking space. Areas we remain excited about include boutique advisory firms poised to benefit from an increase in M&A activity that will accompany the eventual decrease in interest rates. In addition, we see potential opportunities in the alternative asset space as those products continue to grow and become available in the wealth management and retail channels. Lastly, we see what we regard as distinct opportunities within the transaction processor space.

The consumer

The continued, although slowing, moderation in inflation and thus- far steady job market continue to provide support to the consumer. Tariffs have injected some additional uncertainty into the consumer outlook although thus far the overall impacts appear manageable. Against this backdrop, we find a few different dynamics at play:

  • Consumers continue to prioritize travel and experiences,
  • Consumers remain interested in overall health and wellness, and
  • Consumers continue to be quite discerning about where they want to spend their discretionary funds.

Some areas we remain excited about include companies offering exclusive experiences, ranging from cruise vacations to spa treatments while on cruises to trips to amusement parks. We are excited that online sports betting continues to grow with a number of providers approaching profitability. Lastly, we are excited about individual retailers offering distinctive products that we believe can weather the tariff storm.

Risk Considerations:

Investments in small-cap companies generally involve greater risks than investing in larger capitalization companies. Small-cap companies often have narrower commercial markets and more limited managerial and financial resources than larger, more established companies. As a result, their performance can be more volatile and they face greater risk of business failure, which could increase the volatility of a fund’s portfolio. Additionally, small-cap companies may have less market liquidity than larger companies.

Growth companies are expected to increase their earnings at a certain rate. When these expectations are not met, investors may punish the stocks excessively, even if earnings showed an absolute increase. Growth company stocks also typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns.

Investing in mid-cap stocks may involve greater risks than investing in larger, more established companies. These companies often have narrow markets and more limited managerial and financial resources. The companies engaged in the technology industry are subject to fierce competition and their products and services may be subject to rapid obsolescence. The values of these companies tend to fluctuate sharply.

Definitions

Absolute return is the return that an asset achieves over a specified period of time. A relative return is the return an asset achieves compared to a benchmark indicator over a specific period.

Aftermarket refers to parts or accessories that are not produced by an original equipment manufacturer but are designed and sold to be used with or in place of the manufacturer’s parts.

Alternative assets, also known as alternative investments, refer to any investments or asset classes that fall outside the traditional asset classes of stocks, bonds, or cash. They can include hedge funds, private equity, private debt, venture capital, managed futures, derivatives contracts, commodities, cryptocurrency, art and antiques, and, in some cases, real estate. Alternative assets are often less regulated and less liquid than traditional asset classes.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

A beat is when a company’s reported earnings or other business results exceed or are better than the expectations of analysts and others who follow the company’s stock.

Capital expenditures, or capex, are monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

A clinical readout in healthcare is the publication or release of the results from a clinical trial or study of a new medicine, procedure, or healthcare product.

Cyclical stocks have prices influenced by macroeconomic changes in the economy and are known for following the economy as it cycles through expansion, peak, recession, and recovery.

A drawdown is a decline in the returns of a security or group of securities, as measured over a period from the peak of returns to their trough.

Earnings per share (EPS) is calculated as a company’s profit divided by the outstanding shares of its common stock. The resulting number serves as an indicator of a company’s profitability.

Fiscal policy refers to the tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country’s economy.

Generalist investors typically invest in a wider range of industries, sectors, or other areas of the market than specialist investors with a more narrow focus.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is the total value of goods and services provided in an economy during a specified period, often one year.

Growth investing focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance refers statements from the managers of publicly traded companies about their expectations for near-term profits or losses.

Hyperscaler refers to the largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

Liberation Day is a term used by President Donald Trump to refer to April 2, 2025, when he announced a wide range of unexpectedly high tariffs on many U.S. trading partners, triggering a selloff of risk assets.

Macroeconomic refers to the branch of economics that seeks to understand the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Market capitalization, or market cap, refers to the total dollar market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

Momentum stocks benefit from the continuance of an existing and often accelerating market trend. In momentum trading, investors buy securities that are already rising and look to sell them when they look to have peaked.

NATO, or the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, is a political and military alliance of 32 countries from Europe and North America that is organized to guarantee their security and cooperation.

The One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act was passed by Congress in July 2025 to make permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, as well as increase the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, change a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, make changes to health insurance legislation, phase out or reduce credits for clean energy production or use, and remove tax benefits for illegal immigrants, among other things.

OPEC+ consists of OPEC, plus 10 other oil-producing countries (in order of production): Russia, Mexico, Kazakhstan, Oman, Azerbaijan, Malaysia, Bahrain, South Sudan, Brunei, and Sudan. OPEC+ was established in 2016 as rising U.S. shale oil production caused prices to fall dramatically. The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, or OPEC, was founded in 1960 and is a permanent organization of 13 oil-exporting developing nations that coordinates the petroleum policies of its member countries, which are Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, Libya, the United Arab Emirates, Algeria, Nigeria, Gabon, Angola, Equatorial Guinea, and Congo.

Original equipment manufacturers (OEM) make parts for use in other firms’ finished products.

Overhang occurs when investors wait for an expected event or set of circumstances to play out before they are willing to buy a stock.

Secular stocks are characterized by having consistent earnings over the long term constant regardless of other trends in the market. Secular companies often have a primary business related to consumer staples most households consistently use whether the larger economy is good or bad.

Secular trends are large-scale and ongoing changes in economies and societies that have the potential to drive broad and lasting economic, technological, social or other kinds of changes.

A step change is a term used to describe changes that are sudden, large, and/or represent an improvement in existing conditions.

Tailwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment’s performance.

Value investing is an investment strategy that involves picking stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

Verticals refers to lines of business that are focused on specific markets or consumers that are connected around a particular niche.

Visibility reflects the degree to which a company’s management or the analysts who follow it can reliably estimate future near- or long-term performance.

The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of the small-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

data.title: U.S. Bond Market Continues To Trail Foreign Fixed Income
data.description:
Tilting toward foreign bonds remains a winning strategy for US investors this year, based on a set of ETFs through Friday’s close (Aug. 22). The modest gain for a benchmark of US government and investment-grade securities has been no match for offshore bond markets in US dollar terms.

The top performer year to date: government bonds issued by emerging markets governments (EMLC), which is up 13.9%. The rally is far ahead of the US bond market proxy (BND), which clocks in with a moderate 4.9% total return so far in 2025.

Even the weakest foreign bond performer – corporates in emerging markets (EMCB) - is outperforming with a 5.9% rally.

A key factor for the offshore rally is the weak US dollar. The US Dollar Index is down 9.9% year to date. All else equal, a lower greenback translates into higher offshore prices after translating into US dollar terms. Hedging foreign currency risk, in short, has been a hefty headwind this year for US investors investing overseas.

The technical profile for the US Dollar Index still looks bearish, and so the forex tailwind for non-dollar assets remains intact.

Federal Reserve Chairman Powell’s dovish comments last week add another factor for expecting that the greenback will stay relatively weak. The prospect for lower interest rates implies a weaker dollar as the competitive allure declines on the margins for US yields.

Another dimension is the potential that US inflation could heat up due to tariffs. “We see a risk that the Fed could make a policy error by cutting just as activity rebounds, with inflation headed to 3%,” analysts at Bank of America wrote in a research note.

Misguided or not, Fed funds futures are pricing in a high probability (85%) that the Fed will reduce its target rate at next month’s FOMC meeting (Sep. 17). In turn, the odds still look low that US bonds will start to outperform their foreign counterparts in the near term.

data.title: Inside CIBR: What The First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF Offers Investors
data.description:
What Is The First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF?

The First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:CIBR) is an exchange-traded fund that’s all about cybersecurity. It aims to reflect the performance of The Nasdaq CTA Cybersecurity Index, a benchmark tracking companies actively involved in protecting data and digital infrastructure, essentially, businesses that build, manage, or defend against cyber threats. It launched in mid-2015 and carries an expense ratio that’s somewhat higher than broad market index funds, which is par for the course among laser-focused sector ETFs.

What Does CIBR Offer Investors?

For the everyday investor who wants to be part of the cybersecurity growth story without betting on individual stocks, CIBR offers a streamlined, one-ticket solution. However, compared to large-cap tech funds, CIBR is more concentrated, and that brings both opportunity and occasional jolts of volatility. In terms of scale, CIBR manages north of ten billion dollars in assets, and its shares trade quite actively; it’s far from illiquid. It also helps that you’re unlikely to have trouble buying or selling a sizable position at a price that’s pretty close to the fund’s underlying net asset value.

Who Might Consider CIBR?

CIBR suits investors who believe cybersecurity is not just a trend, but a long‑term backbone of the digital economy. If you want exposure to that theme in a way that’s more focused than broader tech funds, but still diversified within the security niche, this ETF checks that box. It may appeal to tactical or momentum-focused investors, too, for example, some traders use technical indicators like reversals and volatility measures to figure out whether to buy and sell CIBR, as it reacts quickly to changes in the cybersecurity market. On the other hand, if you only want to ride the broader tech trend or pay lesser fees and keep your volatility very low, you may want to look elsewhere.

What’s Inside CIBR? A Closer Look at Its Top 10 Holdings

To get a better understanding of the fund’s structure, the following is a break down of CIBR’s top 10 investments.

1. Broadcom Inc – 9.56%

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) has the biggest slice of the CIBR ETF, which says quite a bit about how this fund thinks about “cybersecurity.” Broadcom isn’t a specialist in firewalls or anti-malware software. It’s mostly known for making semiconductors and enterprise tech. Even so, it plays a key part in the machinery that keeps networks running and data flowing, which, when you think about it, is where a lot of security has to begin.

2. Cisco Systems Inc – 8.50%

Cisco Systems Inc. (CSCO) holds a prominent spot in the CIBR ETF, which tells you the fund has a soft spot for big, established tech firms that have been wiring the internet together since dial-up. Cisco started out mostly making hardware, but over time, it’s taken on more of the security and cloud work too. And by giving Cisco a healthy chunk of the portfolio, CIBR is clearly banking on a company that’s managed to stay useful through several tech eras.

3. Palo Alto Networks Inc – 7.61%

Palo Alto Networks Inc. (PANW) is CIBR’s most straightforward play on a company that does cybersecurity and more or less nothing else. Unlike the tech giants in the fund that dabble in all sorts of things, Palo Alto sticks to defending networks, apps, and cloud setups. Its sizable position in the ETF suggests that CIBR still likes the idea of backing firms that stay in their lane.

4. Infosys Ltd ADR – 7.49%

Infosys Ltd ADR (INFY) is somewhat of an outlier in the CIBR lineup. It’s an Indian multinational mostly known for IT services, not for selling cybersecurity products outright. Even so, it ends up in the thick of things, helping companies move to the cloud and manage their tech infrastructure securely. Its decent-sized share in the fund suggests that CIBR is also making room for firms that deliver cybersecurity as part of a larger package, which adds some international reach and a more service-based kind of stability to the mix.

5. CrowdStrike Holdings Inc Class A – 7.02%

CrowdStrike Holdings Inc (CRWD) adds a newer, faster-moving element to the CIBR portfolio. It focuses on cloud-based cybersecurity and has built tools that help companies spot and stop threats in real time. A lot of businesses use its platform, and the company’s quick rise makes it a clear example of the kind of innovation CIBR seems happy to support. Its presence in the fund shows that CIBR is willing to back firms that may be a tad unpredictable but have a lot of potential.

6. Leidos Holdings Inc – 4.86%

Leidos Holdings Inc (LDOS) brings something a little different to CIBR: cybersecurity work tied to government and defense. Unlike most of the other tech firms in the fund, Leidos focuses on public-sector contracts, often dealing with national defense, transportation, and other systems you don’t really want going offline. Its spot in the top ten suggests that CIBR sees value in the kind of security that comes with long-term government work. It also helps steady the fund, adding some reliable, contract-based income to balance out the faster-moving parts.

7. Cloudflare Inc – 4.64%

Cloudflare Inc (NET) sits at the point where internet speed and security meet. It does everything from helping websites load quickly to keeping them from being knocked offline by attacks. Its global network supports millions of sites, making it a sort of behind-the-scenes workhorse of the web. CIBR’s decision to give Cloudflare a solid spot in the portfolio shows it’s interested in companies that protect not just individual devices or data, but the internet itself.

8. CyberArk Software Ltd – 4.54%

CyberArk Software Ltd (CYBR) takes a slightly more focused approach than most of the companies in the fund. It deals mainly with identity and access, making sure people can only get into systems they’re supposed to, and only when they’re supposed to. That’s become a fairly significant part of cybersecurity. Its spot in CIBR’s top ten suggests the fund is paying attention to that shift. It’s also a sign that CIBR isn’t just going after big names, but also companies that do very specific, necessary jobs in keeping systems secure.

9. Gen Digital Inc – 4.26%

Gen Digital Inc (GEN), which used to go by NortonLifeLock, covers the side of cybersecurity that deals with regular people rather than companies. It’s behind some of the better-known antivirus and personal protection software out there, and it makes its money mostly through subscriptions. Its place in CIBR suggests that, while the fund mostly leans toward business-focused firms, it still makes room for companies that cater to everyday users. That adds a dash of balance and gives the fund some exposure to demand that doesn’t come from corporate IT departments.

10. Zscaler Inc – 3.92%

Zscaler Inc (ZS) finishes out CIBR’s top ten and highlights unmistakably where cybersecurity seems to be heading. Its approach, built for the cloud and based on what’s called a zero-trust model, is starting to take over from older, more traditional systems, especially as more companies move to hybrid work and spread their apps across different platforms. Its inclusion in the fund shows that CIBR is thinking ahead. Even though it’s a smaller position, it’s clearly there as a hedge on what comes next, not just what’s worked so far

CIBR’s Performance Overview

CIBR offers something without much ambiguity: strong long-term returns if you’re okay with low income and, to a degree, more volatility than usual. Its total-return numbers back that up. Over the past year, it gained 24.40%, comfortably ahead of the S&P 500 at 17.63% and well above the ETF median of 11.16%, which explains the fund’s “A” grade for momentum in this specific time-frame.

And it’s not just a short-term pop: over three years it’s up 66.83%, and over five years 109.46%, again beating both the market and similar funds. The takeaway: because cybersecurity keeps getting more important, investors who have stuck with this theme have been rewarded.

Strip out dividends and the pattern holds. On a price-only basis CIBR gained 24.07% over one year, 64.95% over three, and 105.43% over five, outpacing the S&P 500’s price returns of 16.09%, 56.28%, and 90.36% across the same periods. That persistent premium suggests the ETF has captured the core drivers of the theme with notable consistency across multi-year windows.

Short-term dynamics are less flattering. Recent momentum has faltered, with a 1-month return of -2.05% that earns an “F” and contrasts with the S&P 500’s 2.49% gain. The implication is not that the long-term thesis is broken, but that a tech-heavy, thematic portfolio does not always lead during rebounds or risk-on bursts; factor whipsaws can leave it lagging even as the cycle turns.

CIBR’s Dividend Profile

Income is where things fall apart. The trailing yield is just 0.26%, far below the 2.67% ETF median and graded “F”. Worse, distributions are shrinking: dividend growth is negative over almost every horizon, at -29.64% year-over-year, -15.13% over three years, and -17.26% over five, producing D- or F-level marks. There haven’t been any consecutive years of dividend growth either, another “F”, though it has at least paid something for nine years running, which earns a “B” for showing up. But if you’re after yield or steady income, CIBR probably isn’t what you’re looking for.

CIBR’s Expense Profile

Costs and trading frictions are mixed, but generally fine. The expense ratio is 0.59%, a little higher than the ETF median of 0.50%, which earns it a “C” and puts it on the more expensive end, but not unusually so for a themed fund. Liquidity, at least, is better with a bid-ask spread of just 0.09%, it gets a “B+,” meaning you can get in or out without much trouble or extra cost.

CIBR’s Risk Profile

Risk is where the trade-offs start to show. CIBR’s standard deviation is 15.12, marginally higher than the 12.68 median, which earns it a “C-.”

Annualized volatility is 24.04% compared to 17.12% for most ETFs, which gets it a “D” and should give conservative investors something to think about.

Tracking precision could be viewed as another concern. The one-year tracking error is 10.25%, noticeably above the 7.41% median, which gets it a “C-”. Over five years, the gap gets wider, 14.85% compared to 8.93%, earning a “D”. Put simply, the fund has often drifted from its benchmark more than investors probably expect from an ETF. That can be off-putting if you’re counting on it to stick close to the index through thick and thin.

Finally, concentration is something to keep an eye on. The top ten holdings make up 62.40% of the fund, quite a bit higher than the 40.22% median, which earns it a “D+”. That kind of focus has worked out so far, the top names have done well, but it also means the fund is more exposed if one or two of them encounter a rough patch.

Should You Invest in CIBR?

In the end, CIBR is a well-defined, high-conviction bet on the long-term growth of cybersecurity. It’s done well over the years, with returns that line up nicely with the theme it’s meant to track. The fund sticks to a mix of companies that build the systems, protect the networks, and handle the cloud. Still, there are trade-offs. The income is low, short-term performance can slip, and it doesn’t always offer the smoothest ride. Add in a tracking error that runs higher than most, and a fairly concentrated portfolio, and what you have is a fund best suited for investors who are comfortable with growth exposure that doesn’t always behave like the broader market. For those who believe cybersecurity is essential and still has room to run, CIBR remains a relevant, if imperfect, way to get there.

This article answers these three main questions about CIBR:

  • Is CIBR geared more towards growth or income investors?
  • Are there concentration concerns with CIBR?
  • How has CIBR performed compared to similar ETFs and the overall market?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Netskope Begins IPO Effort On Growing Revenue, High Losses
data.description:
Netskope Is Growing Amid High Operating Losses

Netskope, Inc. (NTSK) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

NTSK provides data cybersecurity software and related services worldwide.

The company is growing revenue quickly and retention metrics are solid, but operating losses, while dropping, remain very high.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does Netskope Do?

Santa Clara, California-based Netskope, Inc. was founded to develop its SaaS, cloud-based Netskope One integrated security platform to assist organizations in securing their data assets.

Management is led by co-founder, Chairman and CEO Mr. Sanjay Beri, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2012 and was previously in various executive positions at Juniper Networks and was a co-founder of Ingrian Networks, a data protection and privacy solutions company.

The company's primary offerings and capabilities include:

  • Monitoring

  • Response

  • Compliance

  • 120 Edge Data Centers

Netskope has booked fair market value investment of $1.5 billion as of July 31, 2025 from investors, including Lightspeed Venture Partners, ICONIQ and Accel.

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

What Is Netskope’s Market?

According to a 2023 market research report by Grand View Research, the global data-centric security market was an estimated $4.3 billion in 2022 and is expected to exceed $24 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 24.2% from 2023 to 2030, a very high growth rate.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are a continued transition to complex cloud environments and growing threats from various actors, especially entering the generative AI era, which may serve to multiply those threat vectors.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the US data-centric security market by solution type, through 2030:

The firm derives most of its revenue through the indirect channel and the pie charts below show revenue contribution by channel and region:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Netskope:

  • Integrated cloud-native security platform, 120 edge data centers, strong Fortune 100 customer base

  • Heavy dependence on partner ecosystem for sales, high R&D requirement for evolving threat environment

Opportunities

  • Expanding its customer base to include US Federal Govt and mid-market, and grow into adjacent markets via M&A

Threats

Netskope’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:
